Healthy.symptoms_visible: No visible disease symptoms. Normal green leaves, no streaking, lesions, or pustules.
Healthy.prevention_next_season: Maintain field hygiene, balanced fertilization, and regular inspection.

MSV.causal_agent: A geminivirus (Maize streak virus / Maize streak mastrevirus), transmitted by leafhopper vectors. Not spread mechanically or through seed.
MSV.symptoms_visible: Pale spots or flecks (0.5-2mm) appear on leaves 3-7 days after infection. In severe cases these develop into longer streaks that eventually merge. Plants infected before the 4-5 leaf stage can be severely stunted.
MSV.immediate_treatment: There is no direct treatment for infected plants. Remove severely affected plants where practical. Consult your national pesticide guide for approved insecticides targeting the leafhopper vector.
MSV.prevention_next_season: Avoid planting downwind of infected crops and manage rotation carefully. Clear old crop residue after harvest. Use MSV-resistant or tolerant maize varieties where available.

MLB.disease_name: Maize Leaf Blight (Northern Corn Leaf Blight / Turcicum Leaf Blight)
MLB.causal_agent: A fungus, Exserohilum turcicum (also called Setosphaeria turcica), which overwinters in old infected crop debris and spreads via wind- and rain-dispersed spores.
MLB.symptoms_visible: Long, elliptical, cigar-shaped lesions, gray-green turning tan-brown, running parallel to the leaf margins, typically 2-30cm long. First symptoms usually appear around 14 days after infection.
MLB.immediate_treatment: Apply an approved fungicide in severe cases. Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves and residue where practical.
MLB.prevention_next_season: Rotate maize with a non-host crop. Destroy old maize residue after harvest. Use resistant hybrid varieties. Avoid dense planting.

CommonRust.causal_agent: A fungus, Puccinia sorghi, which needs a second host plant (Oxalis species, a common weed) to complete its full life cycle. Spores spread by wind between fields.
CommonRust.symptoms_visible: Small golden-brown, circular to oval powdery pustules (1-2mm) on both leaf surfaces, roughly equal in number top and bottom. Pustules darken toward black later in the season. Often first appears after silking.
CommonRust.immediate_treatment: Fungicide application can help in severe outbreaks, especially on susceptible varieties. Monitor from the whorl stage onward and treat once a large share of leaves show pustules.
CommonRust.prevention_next_season: Use rust-resistant maize varieties -- this is the most effective control. Avoid planting fields with staggered maturity dates near each other. Control nearby Oxalis weeds where practical.

GrayLeafSpot.disease_name: Gray Leaf Spot (Cercospora zeae-maydis / Cercospora zeina)
GrayLeafSpot.causal_agent: A fungus (Cercospora zeae-maydis, or the related Cercospora zeina), which survives in old maize residue and spreads via rain-splashed spores onto lower leaves.
GrayLeafSpot.symptoms_visible: Starts as small pinpoint spots with a yellow halo on lower leaves, best seen held up to light. Spots expand into narrow, rectangular, tan-to-gray lesions running parallel to leaf veins, which can merge and kill entire leaves in severe cases. Usually appears 2-3 weeks before tasseling.
GrayLeafSpot.immediate_treatment: Fungicides applied just before tassel emergence can help where affordable. Consult local guidance on approved products.
GrayLeafSpot.prevention_next_season: Destroy plant residue after harvest through deep plowing. Rotate with a non-host crop. Use more tolerant varieties where available. Widen plant spacing to improve airflow.
